sections.intro: Start with the browser styling pipeline and the cascade rules that decide which declarations survive to computed style.

blocks.title: The browser styling pipeline
blocks.html:
The browser does not jump straight from CSS text to pixels. It parses stylesheets, builds the CSSOM, matches selectors against elements, resolves the cascade, computes values, lays out boxes, paints pixels, and then composites the result. When you know where a decision happens, you know what kind of bug you are fixing.

  • Parsing and selector matching decide which declarations are eligible.
  • The cascade decides which declaration wins.
  • Computed, used, and actual values are resolved later in the pipeline.

items.label: Origin
items.html: Declarations can come from the user agent, the user, the author, or inline style. Origin is the first broad bucket the cascade uses before it looks at anything finer.

items.label: Importance
items.html: Normal declarations and !important declarations live in separate precedence groups. Importance can flip an otherwise obvious winner.

items.label: Order
items.html: When origin, importance, layers, and specificity all tie, the later declaration wins by source order. That is the last tie-breaker, not the first one.

blocks.title: How the browser resolves a declaration
steps: The browser gathers all declarations that match the element and property.
It sorts them by origin and importance before comparing anything else.
Cascade layers are checked next, which can override specificity expectations.
Specificity breaks ties within the same layer and origin bucket.
If everything still ties, source order decides the winner.

blocks.title: Layered cascade example
blocks.caption: Layers give you an earlier control point than selector strength, so a deliberate layer order can prevent specificity arms races.

blocks.title: Drill: which rule wins?
blocks.prompt: A button is styled in @layer components with .button { color: white; }, then a later unlayered rule says button[aria-pressed="true"] { color: gold; }, and the element also has inline style color: teal. Which color wins, and why?
blocks.answerHtml: teal wins if the inline style is a normal declaration, because inline author styles sit above stylesheet author rules in the cascade. If one of the declarations is marked !important, that can change the ordering, so the exact answer still depends on importance before specificity or source order are considered.

blocks.title: Chapter 1 recap
items: The browser styling pipeline moves from parsing and matching to cascade, computation, layout, paint, and compositing.
The cascade considers origin, importance, layers, specificity, and source order in that order.
Layers are a structural tool for avoiding accidental selector wars.
If a style surprise feels mysterious, identify which pipeline stage actually made the decision.

qa.question: What is the CSS cascade?
qa.answerHtml: The cascade is the browser's decision process for choosing which declaration wins when multiple rules target the same property on the same element.

qa.question: What is the browser styling pipeline?
qa.answerHtml: It is the sequence from CSS parsing and selector matching through the cascade, computed values, layout, paint, and compositing.

qa.question: What is the difference between computed and used values?
qa.answerHtml: Computed values are the values after cascade and inheritance have been resolved. Used values are the values after the browser finishes layout and turns percentages, lengths, and other formulas into concrete geometry.

qa.question: Why do CSS layers matter?
qa.answerHtml: Layers let you control broad override order before specificity gets involved, which makes large codebases easier to reason about and reduces the need for selector escalation.

qa.question: Why can inline styles be hard to override?
qa.answerHtml: Inline styles sit high in the author origin precedence, so they often beat stylesheet rules unless a stronger cascade condition such as !important is used.

sections.intro: Use selectors precisely so styles stay expressive without becoming fragile.

blocks.title: Selector precision
blocks.html:
Selectors are contracts between your CSS and the DOM. A good selector says exactly what relationship matters: a direct child, a hovered state, a selected attribute, or a generated fragment like the first line of a paragraph. The more precise the contract, the less likely you are to break it when the markup changes.

  • Use combinators to describe structural relationships.
  • Use pseudo-classes for state and position.
  • Use pseudo-elements for generated parts of an element.

items.label: Descendant vs child
items.html: .card h2 matches any heading anywhere inside the card, while .card > h2 only matches direct children. The child combinator is narrower and usually safer when the markup structure is known.

items.label: Adjacent vs general sibling
items.html: h2 + p only matches the paragraph immediately after the heading, while h2 ~ p matches every later sibling paragraph. Use the narrower version when order is the actual requirement.

items.label: Pseudo-class vs pseudo-element
items.html: Pseudo-classes describe state or position, such as :hover and :nth-child(). Pseudo-elements describe a part of the element or generated content, such as ::before and ::first-line.

blocks.title: Selector examples
blocks.caption: The examples mix structure, state, and generated fragments without making the selector pattern overly broad.

blocks.title: How selector matching works
steps: The browser starts from the rightmost simple selector and looks for matching elements.
If the rightmost selector fails, the whole selector fails immediately.
Combinators then walk upward, sideways, or both to test the rest of the relationship.
Pseudo-classes inspect state or position, while pseudo-elements target generated pieces of the matched element.

blocks.title: Trap: selectors that know too much
blocks.html: Selectors like .page .sidebar ul li a feel descriptive, but they are brittle because they encode too much of the current markup shape. A tiny DOM refactor can break them even when the actual styling intent has not changed.

blocks.title: Drill: identify the match
blocks.prompt: Given <article class="card"><h2>Title</h2><p>Lead</p><button aria-expanded="true"><span class="chevron"></span></button></article>, which of the selectors in the code block match the paragraph and which match the chevron span?
blocks.answerHtml: .card > h2 + p::first-line matches the paragraph, because the paragraph is the direct sibling after the heading. [aria-expanded="true"] > .chevron matches the span inside the button only if the span is a direct child of the expanded element. The state selector and structural selector each have to be satisfied for the rule to apply.

blocks.title: Chapter 2 recap
items: Selectors describe structure, state, and generated fragments.
Combinators should express the narrowest relationship that really matters.
Pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements solve different jobs.
Brittle selectors usually mean the CSS knows too much about the DOM.

qa.question: What is the difference between a pseudo-class and a pseudo-element?
qa.answerHtml: A pseudo-class targets state or position, while a pseudo-element targets a generated part of an element or a specific fragment of its rendered box.

qa.question: What does the child combinator do?
qa.answerHtml: The child combinator > matches only direct children, not deeper descendants.

qa.question: When should you use :where()?
qa.answerHtml: :where() is useful when you want a selector to be expressive but intentionally low specificity, which makes it easier to override later.

qa.question: Why can overqualified selectors become a maintenance problem?
qa.answerHtml: They encode too much DOM detail, so even harmless markup refactors can break styling intent and force unnecessary CSS rewrites.

qa.question: What is the right-to-left matching strategy in selector engines?
qa.answerHtml: The engine usually starts at the rightmost selector because it is the most specific local filter, then walks outward through combinators only for elements that already matched the final part.

sections.intro: Control reuse and override behavior with specificity, inheritance, custom properties, layers, and scope.

blocks.title: Specificity is not a strategy
blocks.html:
Specificity decides ties, but healthy CSS systems try not to lean on it as the main design tool. A stronger mental model is to set up layers, tokens, and scoped boundaries so the right rule wins naturally instead of by accident. When you do that, specificity becomes a final detail instead of the architecture.

  • Specificity compares selector weight inside the same cascade bucket.
  • Inheritance lets some properties flow from parent to child.
  • Custom properties carry values through the tree until they are resolved by var().

items.label: Specificity
items.html: Specificity is a numeric tie-breaker for matching selectors. It does not tell you whether a rule is a good architectural choice, only whether it outranks another rule in the same context.

items.label: Inheritance
items.html: Inheritance is about property value flow from parent to child. It is useful for typographic and token-driven values, but many layout properties do not inherit and must be set explicitly.

items.label: Layers
items.html: Cascade layers let you order whole groups of rules before selector weight is even considered. That makes broad system decisions easier to control than selector escalation.

blocks.title: How custom properties resolve
steps: The browser computes the cascaded value of a custom property on the element.
If the property is not defined there, inheritance may supply a value from an ancestor.
When a normal property uses var(), the browser substitutes the custom property value at computed-value time.
If the substituted value is invalid for the target property, the declaration becomes invalid and the browser falls back according to the property rules.
Layer order is decided before selector strength, so token layers can stay simple while component layers override them deliberately.

blocks.title: Tokens, layers, and scope
blocks.caption: Tokens carry values, layers control broad precedence, and scope keeps component rules local without pushing specificity upward.

blocks.title: Trap: specificity wars
blocks.html: When teams answer every override problem with a longer selector, an ID, or !important, the stylesheet starts to fight itself. The code may still work, but it becomes difficult to predict, hard to refactor, and painful to extend.

blocks.title: Drill: which value is used?
blocks.prompt: A card sets --surface: #111827 in a tokens layer, a component layer sets --surface: #0f172a on .card, and the child uses background: var(--surface, pink). Which value does the child use if both declarations are valid?
blocks.answerHtml: The child uses #0f172a because the component-layer declaration wins over the token-layer declaration before var() is resolved. The fallback only matters if the custom property is missing or invalid at the point where it is used.

blocks.title: Chapter 3 recap
items: Specificity is a tie-breaker, not a design system.
Inheritance and custom properties are different mechanisms with different jobs.
Layers give you a calmer override structure than selector escalation.
Scope helps keep component styling local without relying on heavier selectors.

qa.question: What is specificity in CSS?
qa.answerHtml: Specificity is the weight the browser uses to break ties between competing selectors in the same cascade context.

qa.question: What is inheritance?
qa.answerHtml: Inheritance is the process by which certain CSS property values flow from a parent element to its children when those children do not specify their own value.

qa.question: How do custom properties differ from Sass variables?
qa.answerHtml: Custom properties are resolved by the browser at runtime and can inherit, cascade, and be changed per element. Preprocessor variables are static at build time.

qa.question: Why are CSS layers useful?
qa.answerHtml: Layers let you separate broad concerns such as tokens, base rules, and components so the intended override order is explicit before selector weight matters.

qa.question: What problem does @scope solve?
qa.answerHtml: @scope keeps rules local to a subtree, which reduces the need for high-specificity selectors and makes component styles easier to reason about.

sections.intro: Build strong layout intuition around the box model, sizing rules, overflow, and positioning.

blocks.title: Geometry before decoration
blocks.html:
The box model turns CSS into geometry. Every element has content, padding, border, and margin, and layout bugs often come from forgetting which part of that box a rule is actually changing. Positioning then decides where that box sits relative to its containing block, its normal flow, or the viewport.

  • Padding changes internal space.
  • Border adds visible thickness around the content box.
  • Margin creates external separation between boxes.

items.label: content-box
items.html: The declared width applies to the content box, so padding and border add to the final rendered size. This is the default, but it is often awkward for app layout.

items.label: border-box
items.html: The declared width includes padding and border, which usually makes component sizing easier to predict. Many codebases apply it globally for that reason.

blocks.title: How used size and positioning are resolved
steps: The browser starts with the specified width and height, then applies box-sizing, padding, and border rules.
Min and max constraints can clamp the size after the initial value is considered.
Intrinsic content size can influence auto sizes when the element is allowed to shrink or grow.
Positioned elements establish or use a containing block so offsets like top and left have a reference frame.
Sticky positioning responds to scroll position, but only within the element's scroll container and containment rules.

blocks.title: Sizing and positioning example
blocks.caption: Box sizing, offsets, and overflow all interact. A card can be perfectly sized and still clip or misplace its children if the containing block is wrong.

blocks.title: Trap: overflow that hides the real bug
blocks.html: overflow: hidden can make a layout look tidy, but it can also clip tooltips, focus rings, sticky elements, and popovers. If the overflow rule is just hiding a positioning bug, the bug will come back in the next component.

blocks.title: Drill: why is the tooltip clipped?
blocks.prompt: A tooltip is absolutely positioned inside a card, but part of it disappears when the card scrolls into a smaller container. The card has overflow: hidden and position: relative. What is happening, and what is the cleaner fix?
blocks.answerHtml: The tooltip is being clipped by the card's overflow boundary because the card is acting as the containing block and the overflow setting is cutting off anything that extends outside the box. A cleaner fix is usually to move the overlay to a higher layer or portal it outside the clipping container instead of hiding the overflow symptom.

blocks.title: Chapter 4 recap
items: The box model is content, padding, border, and margin.
border-box usually makes component sizing easier to reason about.
Positioning depends on containing blocks and scroll containers.
Overflow can fix one layout symptom while creating another.

qa.question: What is the box model?
qa.answerHtml: The box model is the set of boxes that make up an element: content, padding, border, and margin.

qa.question: What does box-sizing: border-box do?
qa.answerHtml: It makes the declared width and height include padding and border, which usually makes sizing more predictable.

qa.question: What is a containing block?
qa.answerHtml: A containing block is the reference box used to resolve offsets and percentages for positioned elements.

qa.question: Why can overflow create bugs?
qa.answerHtml: Overflow can clip content, hide focus indicators, interfere with sticky positioning, and mask the fact that the element was sized or placed incorrectly.

qa.question: Why is sticky positioning tricky?
qa.answerHtml: Sticky depends on both normal flow and scroll container behavior, so it only works as expected when the ancestor overflow and containment rules support it.

sections.intro: Use Flexbox for one-dimensional distribution and alignment.

blocks.title: Flexible rows and columns
blocks.body: Describe how the main axis and cross axis shape everyday UI composition.

sections.intro: Use Grid for explicit two-dimensional page and component layout.

blocks.title: Track-based layout
blocks.body: Introduce rows, columns, placement, and subgrid without adding legacy cards.

sections.intro: Make layouts adapt across viewport sizes, writing modes, and component containers.

blocks.title: Responsive strategy
blocks.body: Reserve media queries, logical properties, and container queries for the skeleton stage.

sections.intro: Shape the visual language with typography, color systems, backgrounds, and effects.

blocks.title: Visual styling basics
blocks.body: Leave room for deeper color and type systems while keeping the chapter navigable.

sections.intro: Add motion thoughtfully with transforms, transitions, animations, and motion tokens.

blocks.title: Motion foundations
blocks.body: Keep the motion chapter as a roadmap for later examples and patterns.

sections.intro: Organize CSS for scale using resets, tokens, layers, and system-level conventions.

blocks.title: System architecture
blocks.body: Reserve space for resets, token design, and scalable conventions.

sections.intro: Understand rendering cost so we can make CSS faster without premature micro-optimization.

blocks.title: Performance model
blocks.body: Introduce layout, paint, and composite cost as the basis for later tuning.

sections.intro: Make CSS resilient for keyboard users, assistive tech, and user preference settings.

blocks.title: Resilient defaults
blocks.body: Keep accessibility and preference-aware styling visible from the start.

sections.intro: Explore newer platform CSS capabilities that reduce JavaScript and integrate with native UI primitives.

blocks.title: Platform features
blocks.body: Hold space for anchor positioning, popover-adjacent CSS, and other platform features.

sections.intro: Close with trade-offs, debugging instincts, and interview-ready recall for the CSS ecosystem.

blocks.title: Capstone interview bank
blocks.body: Keep a representative slice of the legacy Q&A here while the skeleton is still being rebuilt.

qa.question: What is the cascade in CSS?
qa.answerHtml: The cascade decides which declaration wins by considering importance, origin, layers, specificity, and source order.

qa.question: What is the difference between display: none and visibility: hidden?
qa.answerHtml: display: none removes the element from layout, while visibility: hidden keeps its space but hides it visually.

qa.question: What is the box model?
qa.answerHtml: The box model is content, padding, border, and margin.

qa.question: What is the difference between margin and padding?
qa.answerHtml: Padding sits inside the border and adds space within the element; margin sits outside the border and adds space between elements.

qa.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and Grid?
qa.answerHtml: Flexbox is primarily one-dimensional, while Grid is two-dimensional.

qa.question: What are pseudo-classes?
qa.answerHtml: Pseudo-classes target elements based on state or position, such as :hover, :focus, and :nth-child().

qa.question: What are logical properties?
qa.answerHtml: Logical properties adapt to writing mode and direction, using inline and block concepts instead of physical sides.

qa.question: What is a Block Formatting Context (BFC)?
qa.answerHtml: A BFC isolates layout behavior so floats are contained and margin-collapsing is constrained.

qa.question: What is the gap property?
qa.answerHtml: gap controls spacing between grid or flex items without adding margins to the children.

qa.question: How do CSS animations perform vs JavaScript animations?
qa.answerHtml: CSS transforms and opacity can stay on the compositor, while JavaScript often competes for the main thread.

qa.question: What is subgrid?
qa.answerHtml: subgrid lets a child grid inherit track sizing from its parent grid.

qa.question: What is CSS containment?
qa.answerHtml: Containment limits how much of the page the browser must consider when an element changes.
